title: Allocation Formulas, Overflow, and Capacity Proofs · Mastery Check
summary: A safety board presents unfamiliar allocation formulas, overflow, and capacity proofs decisions across code, diagrams, diagnostics, and stakeholder evidence; each distractor corresponds to a named misconception. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for overflow-safe grid allocator. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Prove count times element-size representability before calling allocation functions.
Check header, padding, alignment, terminator, and payload additions in a safe order.
Characterize whether the target calloc detects product overflow while retaining caller-side policy and tests.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Capacity Model Proof
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz native-systems team handles memory case 9635. Produce bounded C evidence to prove a header-plus-payload allocation formula; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Derive maximum capacity from object limit, element size, metadata, and stakeholder workload before allocation.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?

Evidence target: Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Capacity Model Proof.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for derive maximum capacity from object limit, element size, metadata, and stakeholder workload before allocation. For Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Capacity Model Proof, keep Capacity Model Proof connected to executable C memory invariant and explicit native transfer evidence.
